The Wanderer and The Seafarer both speak from exile, but the speakers' dispositions are not the same. Pin the line in each where the controlling figure of exile is most explicit, and identify what each speaker has decided about it.

Iqbal's khudi insists on the self; Ghalib's poetry often empties it. Find an Iqbal verse that reads as a direct rebuke to Ghalibian self-effacement, and decide whether Iqbal has answered Ghalib or talked past him.

Fichte's Zurückforderung argues for thought-freedom against princes. Compare the rhetorical posture with Dunoyer's defense of liberty in Nouveau traité d'économie Vol I.
The saqi recurs across Ghalib and Iqbal but the figure is not idle in either. Find the heaviest saqi-couplet in each — and say what the saqi is being asked to do, beyond pouring wine.
Iqbal writes 'Tarana-e-Hindi' (children's anthem celebrating Hindostan) and 'Tarana-e-Milli' (anthem for the Muslim community). Compare the two anthems — has the political imagination shifted?
Cynewulf's Andreas and Cynewulf's Elene both stage saint vs. pagan confrontations. How does the evangelical strategy differ?
Comte's Traité de Législation Vol IV examines slavery's effects across Greek, Roman, and modern societies. Compare his treatment of Greek vs. Roman slavery — where does he find the deeper corruption?
Iqbal's 'Husn o Ishq' (beauty and love) and 'Haqeeqat-e-Husn' (the reality of beauty) function as companion pieces. Lay out the doctrinal argument across both.
Manu and Viṣṇu both list the mahāpātakas. Pull both lists. Are the same five sins named, in the same order? If not, which text dropped, added, or rearranged?
Take Ghalib's couplet on the heart as 'naala' (lament made physical) and Iqbal's lament-imagery in 'Nawa-e-Gham'. How does each figure transform?
Bang-e-Dara Part 1 contains Iqbal's children's-poem cycle (Bachay ki Dua, Bacha aur Shama, Maa ka Khwam, Parinde ki Faryad). What pedagogical project unifies them?

'Shama' alone is Iqbal's candle-poem; 'Shama o Parwana' adds the moth. Pull both poems. What does the addition of the moth do to the metaphysics of the candle — does the moth complete the figure or break it?

Pick one specific procedural rule that appears in both Naradasmriti and Bṛhaspati Smṛti — say, the rules around oaths, or the rules around witnesses. Quote the operative line from each, and say whether Bṛhaspati's version reads as a clarification, a tightening, or a departure from Nārada's.
Vrhaspatitattva (Old Javanese) and Brhaspati Smriti (Sanskrit) both bear the same authorial name. Are these the same Brhaspati? Compare contents to argue the case.
Iqbal's 'Aaftab' (Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 14) and 'Mehr-e-Roshan' (ch. 32) both name the sun. What is each poem actually doing differently with the figure?
How does Manusmṛti book 1 open, and how does Yājñavalkya Smṛti book 1 open? Pull each frame and ask what each text is committing itself to in those opening verses.
How does Manu's account of the four ages (yugas) compare with what Visnu Smriti says about the same sequence?

In the Traité de la propriété, Comte argues for property's natural origin in Vol I and develops the argument in Vol II. Find the moment in Vol II where the argument first turns toward intellectual property and trace the move.

Trace Comte's argument across Volumes I to IV of the Traité de Législation: where does the argument become specifically about slavery, and at what point does it leave the abstract for the historical case studies?
Iqbal returns to the star — 'Sitara', 'Subah ka Sitara', 'Do Sitare' — across the three Bang-e-Dara volumes. Pull each star-poem and trace what the figure carries from one to the next.
How does Iqbal's 'Chand' in Part 3 differ from the moon-figure in 'Maah-e-Nau' (Part 1 ch. 30)?

Ghalib and Zauq both served at the Mughal court — Zauq as poet-laureate, Ghalib as a rival. Find a couplet from each that registers court life directly, and contrast the tone — wry, deferential, bitter, ironic?
The rind — the libertine drinker — figures in both Ghalib and Zauq. Pull a rind-couplet from each. Whose rind is the more theological figure, and whose the more social?

Iqbal in 'Saqaliya' mourns Sicily; in 'Bilad-e-Islamiya' he surveys Islamic cities; in 'Goristan-e-Shahi' he walks among Mughal tombs. Construct the political-aesthetic argument running through these three poems.

Juliana confronts a demon; Andrew confronts the Mermedonians. Both Cynewulf scenes stage interrogations across an ontological gap. Pull a representative interrogation moment in each and ask: who is the more skilled interrogator?

'The Wife's Lament' and 'The Husband's Message' look at the same exile-separation from two sides. Pull the most distinctive line from each speaker. Are these two voices in dialogue, or speaking past each other?

Comte and Dunoyer both write about the 'classes industrieuses' but they classify them differently. Find Comte's discussion in the Traité de Législation and Dunoyer's parallel treatment, and identify the principle each uses to draw class boundaries.
Manusmriti chapter 1 narrates creation; Yajnavalkya's chapter 1 opens differently. How does Yajnavalkya frame the law's authority instead?
Iqbal's Bang-e-Dara is divided into three parts written in different decades. Comparing Part 1 and Part 3, what political shift do you detect in Iqbal's lyrical 'we'?
Find every poem in Bang-e-Dara that explicitly addresses the Prophet, then say whether they form a coherent doctrinal cluster or vary by occasion.

Manu and Viṣṇu both walk through the Brahmin's daily ritual obligations. Pick one part of the day — say morning purification — and find the rule for it in each text. Where does Viṣṇu add detail Manu skips?
Iqbal's 'Falsafa-e-Gham' offers a philosophy of grief; Ghalib's diwan resists philosophy and dwells on grief itself. Pick a Ghalib couplet and an Iqbal passage and stage the disagreement.
Manusmriti chapter 5 and Visnu Smriti both treat permitted and forbidden foods. Where does Manu's vegetarian leaning push past Visnu's allowances?
Yajnavalkya Smriti is famous for being more concise than Manu. Pick a topic Manu treats expansively and show the Yajnavalkya version side by side.

The sea figures in both Elene and Andreas, but the texts use it differently. Pull a sea-passage from each and ask what the sea is doing in each — testing the saint, carrying him, separating him from rescue?
How do 'The Wanderer' and 'Soul and Body II' each handle the question of what survives the body? Compare their answers.
Compare what Comte says about religion as a foundation for legislation (Traité de Législation Vol I ch. 8) with what Fichte says against princely religious authority in Zurückforderung.
Cynewulf's three signed poems (Andreas, Elene, Juliana) all stage suffering for faith. Compare the three protagonists' encounters with violence — where does each give and where does each withhold?

Manu and Yajnavalkya both treat women's autonomy in property. The famous Manu line is that 'a woman is never independent'. Find that line, then find Yajnavalkya's parallel rule. Does Yajnavalkya endorse, soften, or quietly diverge from it?
Iqbal's Part 2 contains 'Tanhai' and 'Talaash'. Read them as paired states (solitude / quest) — how does each prepare for the other?

Comte treats writing as evidence in Traité de Législation. Bṛhaspati Smṛti introduces lekhya as a vyavahara category. Pull the operative claim on each side. Does either tradition imagine writing as more or less reliable than oral testimony?

'Bachay ki Dua' is a child's prayer; 'Maa ka Khwam' is a mother's dream. Both poems work the parent-child relation, but from different ends. Pin the line in each that most charges the relation, and ask: are these two voices about the same relationship, or two different ones?
Iqbal's 'Ek Makra aur Makhi' and 'Ek Pahar aur Gilehri' are both fable-poems. What common ethical structure underlies them?
Where in Manu and where in Visnu do you find the same prescription about the king's eight ministers? Does the wording match, or does each adapt?

Yajnavalkya and Viṣṇu both treat naimittika (occasional) duties. Pull each text's death-ritual (asauca) timeline. Where do the prescribed periods diverge, and what does the divergence imply about each text's social model?
How does Comte's stadial history (Traité de Législation Vol II) read alongside Dunoyer's stages of liberty (Vol I)? Where do their typologies converge?
Take a Ghalib couplet on wine and a Zauq couplet on wine — what differs in how each treats intoxication?

Iqbal's early 'Mere' and his later self-addresses in Part 3 are both first-person. Pull a representative line from each. Has the speaking 'I' actually changed, or has only its rhetorical surroundings changed?
How does the Cynewulf of Elene treat Helena's authority compared with the way Juliana treats its female protagonist? Both centre a saintly woman.

Niyoga (levirate) is mentioned in both Manu and Yajnavalkya, but they do not treat it the same way. Pull each text's rule and decide which is closer to approval and which to permission-with-reluctance.
Iqbal's 'Insaan' poems in Part 2 and Part 3 (same title) reach different conclusions about the human being. Pick a line from each that captures the divergence.

Both speakers — the Wanderer and Ghalib's persona in 'Hazaron khwahishen aisi' — sit with the residue of unmet desire. Pull a line for each and say which speaker would find the other's grief recognisable.
Manu, Visnu, Yajnavalkya, and Narada all weigh in on adultery (paradarika). What is the trajectory of severity across the four?
How does Ghalib's deployment of 'fanaa' (annihilation) compare with Iqbal's reframing of 'fanaa' as unfit for a Muslim self?
Iqbal's 'Husn o Ishq' (Part 2 ch. 13) versus Ghalib's many couplets where husn and ishq are tested against each other. Where does Iqbal explicitly resolve what Ghalib leaves unresolved?

Comte argues ancient slavery distorted later legal systems; Dunoyer argues slavery degrades industry. Find each author's strongest formulation. Are they making the same case, or are these two different objections to slavery sharing a common surface?
How does Cynewulf's runic signature pattern (in Elene and Juliana) relate to the closer-to-anonymous practices of the other Old English elegies? Compare modes of authorial presence.
Iqbal in 'Shikwa' addresses God with grievance. In 'Falsafa-e-Gham' he philosophises grief. How does the rhetorical move differ?
Manu and Yajnavalkya both treat the four āśramas. Pull each text's account. Are the boundaries between āśramas drawn in the same place, or has one text shifted them?

Manu's book 1 makes caste cosmogonic — varnas emerge with creation. Slokantara, in its Old Javanese gnomic register, treats social distinction more pragmatically. Pull a representative passage on each side. Is Slokantara presupposing Manu's framework or working with a different one?
Vrhaspatitattva and Ganapatitattva are Old Javanese tattva texts. What do they share doctrinally, and where does each pull away from the other?
How do Kalpabuddha and San Hyan Kamahayanikan differ in their handling of the Mahayana doctrine of the bodhisattva path?
San Hyan Tattvajnana and San Hyan Mahajnana are both 'jnana' (knowledge) texts in Old Javanese. What different epistemological angle does each take?

Kalpabuddha treats kewala-jñāna; San Hyan Mahājñāna treats the highest goal. Pull the central claim from each. Are these two texts naming the same end-state, or is one's terminus the other's milestone?
Iqbal's 'Bachay ki Dua' is a child praying. Manu's chapter 2 prescribes the brahmacari's prayers. Compare the implicit pedagogies.
Comte and Fichte both insist that legitimate authority is constrained. Where in Comte (Traité de Législation Vol I) does this match Fichte's claim that thought-freedom precedes legitimate rule?

The Wanderer and Resignation both speak of surrender to fate, but 'resignation' means different things in each. Pull the moment of surrender in each. Whose resignation is closer to consent, and whose closer to defeat?
Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' (Part 3 ch. 25) critiques territorial nationalism. Earlier in Part 1, his 'Tarana-e-Hindi' celebrates Hindostan. Stage the contradiction with quotes.
Find a couplet in Ghalib's diwan and a Zauq couplet that share roughly the same metaphor (e.g. mirror, lamp, wine). Pin them and explain the divergence.
Manu chapter 12 and Yajnavalkya chapter 3 both close with eschatological / liberation material. Compare how each text exits its argument.
Iqbal's 'Insaan' (in Part 3) and Manu's account of the human being in chapter 1 could be staged as two answers to 'what is a human?' What do they each privilege?

Comte's foundational position on liberty appears at the front of Traité de la propriété Vol I. Dunoyer states his at the front of Nouveau traité. Pull each. Whose definition is broader — and whose has more falsifiable conditions attached?

Both Iqbal in 'Sair-e-Falak' and the Wanderer in the Old English elegy move through cosmic geography, but they are looking for different things. Pull the central vision-moment in each — what is each speaker hoping to find?
Take two of Ghalib's couplets on the heart and contrast them. He uses dil more than 200 times across the diwan. Find an example where dil is precious and one where dil is disposable.
Manu's account of the Brahmin's dignity (chapter 1) and Iqbal's address to the Brahmin in 'Sach Keh Doon Ae Brahman' — pin the doctrinal collision and stage it.
Take Cynewulf's Andreas (the apostle abroad) and Iqbal's 'Ek Haji Madinay ke Raastay Mein' (the pilgrim on the road). Both are journey-of-faith poems across cultures.
Iqbal's 'Maa ka Khwam' (a mother's dream) and the Old English 'Wife's Lament' (a woman's exile) both centre a woman's interior. Compare the affective registers.

Manu's book 7 sets out legitimate kingship, and Viṣṇu has a parallel chapter on rājadharma. Pull the qualifying conditions from each. Where does Viṣṇu lean on Manu, and where does Viṣṇu's framing diverge?
Within the Cynewulf cycle, contrast Juliana's prison demon with Andreas's Mermedonian captors. Both are agents of evil but figured very differently.

Iqbal's 'Khitāb ba Jawānān-e-Islām' addresses the young Muslim. Manu's book 2 sets out the brahmacārin. Pull the most demanding line each places on its young man. Could either young man be expected to live up to the other's text?
Manu chapter 5 prescribes mourning periods. Yajnavalkya parallels but with shorter durations. Visnu prescribes still differently. Lay the three side by side.

Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I and Vol II both discuss indigenous occupation of land, but Vol II adds nuance. Find the passage in Vol II that introduces the new wrinkle, and identify what Vol I had been missing.

'Hāh' and 'Nawā-e-Gham' come from the same volume of Bang-e-Dara and share a lament-register. Pull the most charged line in each. Where does the rhetoric coincide, and where does each poem stake out its own territory?
Find what Iqbal says about 'koshish' (effort) and what Ghalib says about effort. Whose disposition toward exertion is more romantic, whose more sceptical?
Iqbal's Saqaliya is a city-elegy; the Old English 'The Ruin' is also a city-elegy. Compare the architectures of loss.

Ghalib's matla — 'Hazaaron khwāhishein aisi ki har khwāhish pe dam nikle' — pictures a desire-cosmology in which each desire is fatal. Iqbal's 'Tere ishq ki intehā chāhtā hoon' wants intensity, not annihilation. Pull both opening couplets and walk through how their desire-cosmologies differ.

Cynewulf signs his name into Elene by hiding runes. Iqbal signs himself in maqtas, by name. Pull each signature passage and ask: is each poet signing for the same purpose — claim, memory, license, vanity — or for different ones?
Take the Ghalib couplet about the heart's brick-and-stone (Dil hi to hai) and pair it with an Iqbal verse where the heart is similarly objectified. Stage the conversation.
Iqbal in 'Wataniyat' attacks territorial nationalism. Comte in his Traité de Législation Vol II discusses national identity differently. Where could one stage a productive disagreement?
Old English 'The Wanderer' moves from particular lament to universal homiletic. Iqbal's 'Falsafa-e-Gham' moves from question to philosophy. Compare how each rhetorical arc is built.
Iqbal's 'Bachay ki Dua' versus 'Maa ka Khwam' — what does the corpus say about how parental and child voices speak past each other?

Manu, Yājñavalkya, and Viṣṇu each open by establishing the source of dharmic authority. Pull each opening. Whose source-derivation is the most genealogical, whose the most procedural — and which is hardest to argue with?

Manu's chapter 1 treats varna cosmogonically; Yajnavalkya's opening treats varna more procedurally. Pull both openings. Has Yajnavalkya genuinely shifted the framework, or simply stripped the cosmogonic prelude?
Iqbal's 'Insaan' poems differ across volumes. The Part 2 'Insaan' seems pessimistic; the Part 3 one seems prouder. Justify with passages.
Old English 'Resignation' and Iqbal's 'Falsafa-e-Gham' both confront sustained suffering. What posture does each recommend?

The Seafarer pivots to prayer at its close; Cynewulf's runic epilogues do something similar. Pull each closing turn. Are these two devotional closes doing the same theological work, or is one's prayer richer than the other's?

How do Comte's chapters on civil law (Traité de Législation Vol I ch. 6) and on legislation framed as morality (ch. 9) interact? Where does Comte fold morality into civil law and where keep them apart?
Compare Manu's verses on kshatriya duties (chapter 7) with what Yajnavalkya says about the king's behaviour at court.
Comte argues that liberty grounds property; Fichte argues that thought-freedom grounds legitimate authority. Are these the same kind of argument?
Manu and Viṣṇu both treat śrāddha. Pull each text's instructions on the foods used and the days designated. Where does Viṣṇu specify what Manu leaves general?

Iqbal's children's-poems in Bang-e-Dara Part 1 and the Old English gnomic verse in Maxims I are both pedagogical. Pull a representative piece from each. Are these doing the same kind of moral instruction, or are children's-poems a softer form?

Comte and Dunoyer both treat the relationship between morals and laws. Pull each one's strongest formulation. Whose account allows more independence between the two domains — and whose is more vulnerable to the objection that the dependency runs the other way?

Iqbal's 'Sair-e-Falak' speaker enters the heavens; the Wanderer's speaker contemplates a fallen earthly world. Pull what each speaker sees. Why does each see the cosmos this way — what about the speaker has determined the field of view?
Compare Manu's discussion of stridhana (women's separate property) in chapter 9 with Yajnavalkya's treatment in chapter 2.

Iqbal's 'Khitāb ba Jawānān-e-Islām' uses the second person to demand action; Cynewulf's epilogues do the same. Pull each address. Does either author trust the second-person to do the work, or do they hedge by also explaining?
Take Iqbal's 'Sayed ki Loh-e-Turbat' (Sir Sayed's tomb-inscription) and Old English 'The Wanderer' on lost lords. Both elegise a leader. How do the elegies differ?
Yajnavalkya gives a tighter, sutra-style code; Manu gives expansive verse. Pick the same procedural rule in both texts and lay them side by side.

Manu, Yājñavalkya, and Nārada each define sākṣya, lekhya, and divya — what counts as legitimate evidence. Pull each text's rules and identify the order of preference. Where does Nārada's procedure diverge most sharply?

Helena and Juliana both confront male authority, but the authority each confronts is different. Pull the central confrontation scene in each. What kind of authority is each saint refusing — political, sexual, theological, all three?
Iqbal mentions Hindi figures (Brahman, Ram, Hindostan, Hindu sages) in several poems. Where does he engage warmly and where polemically?

Comte Vol II opens with perfectionnement et dégradation; Vol I introduces a different framing. Pull both openings. Has the project's emphasis genuinely shifted, or is Vol II only sharpening what Vol I had already implied?
Iqbal's 'Subah ka Sitāra' celebrates the morning star; 'Maāh-e-Nau' celebrates the new moon. Pull the central image in each. What does dawn promise that the new moon does not?

Iqbal's 'Visal' (union with the beloved) and Ghalib's 'Ye na thi hamari qismat ke wisaal-e-yaar hota' (union denied) — set the two side by side as the affirmative and negative theology of mystical union.

Juliana confined to a cell; the speaker of 'The Wife's Lament' confined under earth. Both are confinement scenes; the framings are very different. Pull the strongest line in each. Does either framing leave room for the other's mode of confinement?

Manu's book 1, Viṣṇu's openings, and Widsith (in the Old English Elegies) all use catalogue. Pull a stretch of catalogue from each. What does cataloguing accomplish in each text — authorising, instructing, mourning, displaying skill?
Comte and Dunoyer both treat climate's effect on national character. Pull each one's strongest formulation. Where do they agree, and where would Comte say Dunoyer is overweighting climate?
How does Iqbal's 'Tarana-e-Hindi' praise of Hindostan compare with the territorial-pride material in Cynewulf's Andreas (when Andrew speaks of his land)?
Manu chapter 11 (prayascittas) and Yajnavalkya chapter 3 (prayascitta) overlap. Find a single offence treated in both and contrast the prescribed expiation.

Widsith catalogues the halls and lords the scop has known. Iqbal in 'Tarana-e-Hindi' catalogues the geography of Hindostan. Pull a representative section from each — what does cataloguing accomplish for each speaker?

Manu and Yajnavalkya both treat the Brahmin's daily routine. Pull each text's rule for the first action of the day. Are the two texts disagreeing on what should be done first, or only on how to describe the ordering?

'Maa ka Khwām' and 'The Wife's Lament' are both women's intimate voices but differently constructed. Pull the most intimate line in each. Which voice is more constructed — that is, more visibly mediated by a male author imagining a female speaker?

Andreas speaks back to Christ-as-helmsman; Helena speaks back to the Jewish elders; Juliana speaks back to Heliseus and to the demon. Three different power-relations. Pull a representative speech-back moment in each, and ask which saint pays the highest price.
Iqbal addresses the Prophet in 'Huzoor Risalat-Maab Mein' and addresses the Brahmin in 'Sach Keh Doon Ae Brahman'. Compare the etiquette of each address.

Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I and Viṣṇu Smṛti each treat ownership of land. Pull each one's foundational statement. What metaphysical assumption does each make about the relation of person to soil?

Ghalib's nightingale (bulbul) and Iqbal's bird in 'Parinde ki Faryād' are both lyric birds, but they sing different things. Pull a Ghalib bulbul-couplet and the operative Iqbal verse. Whose bird sings, whose complains, and what does each register about the speaker?
Manu treats the Brahmin's authority as descended from the Veda; Iqbal in 'Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam' grounds Muslim youth in their ancestors' victories. Compare the appeals to source-authority.
Within Cynewulf's signed corpus, compare the runic signature in Juliana with the runic signature in Elene. Both spell out CYNEWULF in runes; the framing devices differ.

Comte argues ancient slavery distorts later jurisprudence. The smriti tradition has elaborate provisions for dāsa and dāsī. Pull each side. Are Comte and the smritis describing the same phenomenon at different distances, or different phenomena under one English word?
Two Iqbal pieces speak of light: 'Mehr-e-Roshan' (the bright sun) and 'Aaftab' (the sun). What does each light figure?
Iqbal's 'Phool ka Tohfa Ata Hone Par' (on receiving a flower as gift) sits in Part 3 — a tender moment in an otherwise programmatic volume. Compare with the lighter children's-poems of Part 1.

Manu uses 12 chapters; Yājñavalkya uses three structural divisions (ācāra, vyavahāra, prāyaścitta). Pull each architecture and ask what the simplification gains in clarity and what it costs in cosmological framing.

Iqbal addresses the Prophet directly in 'Huzūr Risālat-Maāb'. Cynewulf's Andrew finally recognises Christ in Andreas. Pull each address. Does either poet manage to break out of the conventions of devotional address, and if so, where?
Comte's chapters arguing against religion as the foundation of legislation (Vol I ch. 8) and against legislation grounded in scripture (ch. 9) form a cluster. What's the cumulative claim?

'Maxims I' and 'Vainglory' are both gnomic Old English pieces. Pull the central vice each names. What different vices does each tradition single out — and what does the difference imply about the audience each imagines?

Manu's book 4 sets out the snātaka's life; Viṣṇu's parallel chapter does the same. Pull each rule on a single specific topic — say, what to do when meeting a stranger — and decide whether Viṣṇu rules against what Manu permits.
Iqbal's 'Ek Pahar aur Gilehri' (mountain and squirrel fable) and the Old English 'Maxims I' both extract general maxims from particulars. Compare the fabular logics.
Iqbal addresses the youth of Islam (Khitab) and the Brahmin (Sach Keh Doon). Manu chapter 7 addresses the king. Comte addresses legislators. Compare the four 'addressee-shaping' rhetorical postures.
Compare how Manu treats unintentional killing in chapter 11 with how Yajnavalkya treats it in chapter 3. Same offence, comparable era, somewhat different punishment.
Iqbal's 'Kali' (the bud, Bang-e-Dara Part 2 ch. 16) and 'Phool ka Tohfa Ata Hone Par' (on receiving a flower) are flower-themed. Compare their botanies.

Comte derives property from liberty's exercise on territory; Manu's book 7 frames the king's relation to land in dharmic terms. Pull each foundational claim. The starting points differ; where does the resulting royal authority differ?

Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' rejects territorial nationalism; Comte argues territorial nation-building can produce moral progress. Pull each operative claim. Where is the disagreement substantive, and where is it just a difference of vocabulary?
'The Ruin' contemplates a fallen Roman city without naming it. Iqbal's 'Saqaliya' names Sicily. Find the most charged line in each. What does naming or refusing-to-name commit each speaker to?

Which work in the corpus contains a saint who addresses a marble pillar inside a heathen city, calling it 'better than gold' because the King of glory once inscribed the Ten Commandments on it and gave it to Moses (and 'as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it… Joshua and Tobias'), then commands it to spew a 'wide-running water for the ruin of men' upon the corrupt folk? Quote the saint's address to the stone and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus stages a dialogue at sea in which a saint, not yet recognizing that his helmsman is the disguised Lord of glory, praises him as a wave-rider who is 'young, not old in winters' yet steers a 'foam-necked' ship that fares 'most like a fowl' — and then says he himself has been 'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet has never seen such a steersman? Quote the saint's praise of the helmsman and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus narrates a saint breaking into a cannibal city's prison after seven gate-guards drop dead at his approach, embracing a fellow apostle inside, and then leading out a precisely numbered company of captives — 'one hundred and two… and also forty' men, plus 'of women there yet, in addition to the host, one less than fifty'? Quote the verses giving these numbers and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus rules that if a Brahmin's top-knot is cut by enemies five times, his Brahminhood is irrecoverably lost — explicitly stating that even a fresh saṃskāra cannot restore it — while in earlier passages it had laid out that for a single cutting one wears hair from a cow's tail on the ear and performs a Prājāpatya penance, and for cutting in the middle one is again purified by a Prājāpatya? Quote the verses on the fivefold cutting and the cow's-tail remedy verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does a Sanskrit penitential text declare that one who eats in the same row, or together with, a woman who has been married multiple times must be buried covered in a pit dug in the earth for either one watch (three hours) or two ghaṭikās (forty-eight minutes), then perform the birth-rites and other saṃskāras anew, perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances, subsist on barley while performing the Cāpāgrayāna rite, and bathe five thousand times — either himself or through a Brahmin — to be purified for himself alone, the rule attributed to Bhṛgu in ancient times? Quote the passage verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus narrates that the lotus-born creator placed all the divine śrāddha-vegetables on one scale-pan and the jackfruit (panasa) on the other and weighed them with his hand, finding the jackfruit slightly heavier — and then specifies, on that same balance, that the eggplant (bṛhatī) was equal to three hundred, ginger (ārdraka) to six hundred, and sesame (tila) to one hundred, establishing this trio from that day forward as equal to a thousand vegetables for the satisfaction of the ancestors? Quote the weighing and the three numerical equivalences verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit dharmaśāstra, in its enumeration of the twelve kinds of witnesses, describes a category in which a man is hidden behind a wall and made to overhear a statement about a debt, so that — when the debt is later denied — he can come forward and report what he heard? Quote the verse that defines this concealed witness, and name the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit legal treatise warns against forged copies of written instruments by comparing them to a reflection in a mirror — saying that just as a reflection appears real though it is unreal, so skilled persons make copies of documents? Quote the simile and identify the work and chapter section on documents.

Which ancient Indian work on judicial procedure anatomizes the court of justice as a 'ten-limbed instrument' in which the king is the head, the appointed judicial officer is the mouth, the assessors are the arms, the Smṛti is the hands, and the accountant and the scribe together form the shanks? Quote the verse listing these limbs and name the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author ridicule the jurists' theory that property arose from a universal contract among all humans by asking how one would demonstrate to an inhabitant of Peru that he could not pick the fruit at his hand without infringing the rights of an inhabitant of Siberia, or make an inhabitant of Kamchatka understand that he could not clothe himself in an animal's skin without injuring the rights of the Arabs, or persuade the Gauls that they could not in conscience cultivate the plains of Auvergne without first obtaining permission from the inhabitants of Tibet? Quote the cascade of geographic examples and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus argues that the value of land rises in step with civilization by tabulating, from Humboldt's 1803 report, the population per square league across the intendancies of the former kingdom of Mexico in the precise progression '6, 7, 10, 12, 38, 65, 66, 81, 109, 120, 255, 301, 586' — concluding that an extent of land worth six francs in Sonora would be worth two hundred and fifty-five in Mexico and five hundred and eighty-six in Guanaxuato? Quote the line that gives the numerical sequence and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus is Arthur Young's tour of France during 1787, 1788, and 1789 cited as eyewitness testimony to the destruction of the Pyrenees forests — describing men near Bagnères de Luchon cutting and splitting young birches to make barrel hoops, and lamenting that the 'beautiful and noble forest of Lartigues' had been so devastated that within ten or twelve years it would be 'nothing more than a bare mountain, with some miserable shrubs browsed by goats'? Quote the Lartigues passage and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author ridicule the doctrine that every invention is the natural property of its inventor by tracing it to absurd conclusions — that the first man to turn wood into clogs or animal skin into sandals would acquire 'the exclusive right to shoe the human race', so that an inhabitant of the austral pole could not wear clogs without buying them from an inventor at the boreal pole, and that the first cultivator of wheat would have left the rest of mankind reduced to eating snakes, rats, or frogs, or even one another, to remain faithful to the rights of nature? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.

In which work is the modern public debt described as a mechanism by which 'the most intelligent, the most active, the most industrious nation could be transformed into a people of helots, working for a few thousand of the idle' — with the Restoration government's two successive billion-franc loans (one to pay foreigners who had reinstated it, the second to indemnify the émigrés) given as concrete examples of how borrowed capital permanently transfers a nation's revenues to foreign capitalists? Quote the helots line and the passage on the Restoration's two billions, and name the chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author attack the practice of letting books fall into the public domain after a few years' enjoyment by asking why the State, when it needs land for a canal, a great road, or a fortified place, pays the owner an indemnity, but treats writers' heirs as legitimate prey — closing with the rhetorical question whether 'the labors of the writers who have enlightened the world, of Descartes, of Bacon, of Franklin', should be less worthy of protection and respect than the labors of a candlemaker? Quote the Descartes-Bacon-Franklin-candlemaker line and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus is it argued that distinguishing 'arbitrary' from 'legal' rule is largely illusory — because a master can write a perfectly orderly code for governing his slaves and herds, and orderly extortion is still extortion? Quote the passage that makes the analogy and identify the work and chapter.

Which work compares a government that legislates without published rules to a steam engine missing its regulator, and warns that adding a regulator does not change the nature of the machine — only its uniformity? Quote the passage and name the chapter.

In the corpus, where is Chinese infanticide used as a worked example to show that even when a government criminalizes the exposure of children, the actual benefit reaches almost no one — the author estimating that the good 'would perhaps not be felt by one single individual out of two hundred thousand'? Quote the line that gives that ratio and identify the work and chapter.

Which work argues that a civilized people, to be happy, needs only 'not to be pillaged, and to be abandoned to itself' — and that governments are far more powerful at doing harm than at doing good? Quote the line and give chapter attribution.

Where in the corpus does an author argue that Stoic contempt for pain and pleasure was not a metaphysical doctrine but a sociological response — the predictable maxims of men subject to proscriptions, confiscation, and slavery, such that Epictetus's lessons fit a colonial slave as well as a subject of Nero? Quote the line that draws the slave-of-our-modern-colonists comparison, and name the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus closes its first volume by quoting the inscription over the gate of Dante's hell — 'PER ME SI VA NELLA CITTA DOLENTE...LASCIATE OGNI SPERANZA' — to mark the entrance to the career of vices and crimes? Identify the work and chapter, and quote enough of the inscription to confirm the citation.

Which work argues that charitable institutions — foundling hospitals, lying-in houses for unwed mothers, asylums for 'repentant' prostitutes — actually encourage the vices they aim to remedy, because they reduce the natural penalties that would have repressed them while leaving the pleasures intact? Quote the passage that compares such institutions to a society that, out of humanity, would break down the doors of prisons.

Where in the corpus is it argued that the moral and legislative sciences should be modeled on the methods of Adam Smith and J.-B. Say in political economy, treating laws and institutions as facts to be observed rather than systems to be invented? Quote the passage that names Smith and Say in this connection, and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus is a famous philosopher-jurist's climate theory discredited by pointing out that he derived it from an experiment performed on half a sheep's tongue — alternately heating it and freezing it and inspecting the fibers under a magnifying glass — and where the author concludes that 'a moral and political system that rested on such experiments would seem to me to rest on a scarcely solid base'? Quote the passage that describes the half-tongue experiment, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus argues that European colonization in America, southern Asia, the islands of the great Ocean, and southern and western Africa has produced a wholly new kind of social ordering that previous quarrels over rank cannot describe — naming it 'the aristocracy of species' and predicting that it will considerably influence the existence of the American republics? Quote the line that names this new aristocracy and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus are the inhabitants of Tierra del Fuego and the neighboring Patagonians said to have a complexion approaching 'that of iron rust mixed with oil', adduced as evidence — together with their copper-colored kin running all the way to the Saint Lawrence and Bering Strait across one and a half million square leagues — that complexion does not vary with latitude or temperature? Quote the iron-rust-and-oil line and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus is the supposed natural superiority of one race over another rebutted by pointing out that the republic of Athens reportedly had twenty thousand citizens and four hundred thousand slaves — a ratio of twenty to one, the same as that of whites to blacks in the colonies — and that great Romans owned 'up to five hundred, and sometimes even up to a thousand' slaves inside the capital alone, so that 'a twentieth of the men of the Caucasian species was therefore sufficient to maintain the other nineteen-twentieths' in a servitude harsher than that of blacks? Quote the Athens line and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author tell the story of slaves in a Caribbean colony who, when their masters fled a great insurrection, renounced the liberty they could have seized and accompanied their masters in flight to the United States — only to be 'rewarded' by being sold to the first slave traders who presented themselves? In the same passage the author describes blacks in Louisiana whose children, allowed to accumulate a 'peculium,' use their small savings to buy the freedom of their old parents. Quote the line about the masters' reward and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does the author dismantle the 'no genius among the colored species' argument by noting that Roman slaves produced an Epictetus, a Phaedrus, and a Terence, then asks rhetorically what men of genius the Negro slaves of Jamaica or Saint Lucia have produced — only to reply that demanding comedies-of-Terence from black slaves cutting sugar is the same fallacy as concluding the citizens of the United States are a degenerate race because they have produced no orator like Cicero and no poet like Virgil? Quote the Cicero/Virgil line and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus describes a real incident in which slave-owners, organized as a 'secret committee' calling themselves 'respectable gentlemen', issued a proclamation summoning a Sunday mob with 'the necessary tools' to demolish a Methodist chapel, then over two days tore down the building, ripped up Bibles, made flags from the missionary's linen, gave 'three times three' ferocious howls of victory, and afterward published a triumphant proclamation dated from Bridgetown on 21 October 1823? Quote the dated proclamation announcing that 'at midnight they completed the ruin of the church', and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus enumerates the five kinds of papal indulgence-bulls that the Roman clergy delivered to the Spanish government for resale in its American colonies — naming them as 'the bull for the living, that for the dead, that for dairy and eggs, that of composition, and that of the crusade' — and explains that the bull of composition could turn an unjust holder of stolen goods into the 'legitimate owner' provided he handed the indulgence-merchant a small share of his prize? Quote the five-bull enumeration and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus, while arguing that emancipated labor is more productive than coerced labor, claims that 'ten English workers do more work, in a given time, than fifty Russian slaves' and that an English lord who possesses the same extent of land as a Russian lord 'is ten times richer than he, although he does not possess a single slave, while the second possesses thousands'? Quote the comparison and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author pair a remark of Sully to Henri IV — that to subject one's subjects by force of arms one must keep one's 'backside always in the saddle, a hauberk on your back, a helmet on your head, a pistol in your fist, and a sword in your hand' — with a boast attributed to Bonaparte that 'I will go to Madrid, I will go to Vienna with an army of five hundred thousand men,' before observing that with such an army 'one can go to die, captive and forsaken, on a desert rock in the middle of the Atlantic'? Quote the passage and identify the work and chapter.

In the corpus, where is the dynamometer cited as evidence against Rousseau's claim that the savage is physically stronger than the civilized man — with a numerical 'progression' of 50, 51, 58, 69 representing, in order, the natives of Diemen's Land, of New Holland, of Timor, and the French sailors who had just disembarked from a long voyage? Quote the passage that gives this progression, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus reconstructs the etymology of the word 'industry' from the Latin INDU (for INTUS, 'within') joined to STRUO ('I arrange, I set up, I construct'), and uses this philology to argue that the term was first applied to the cunning of swindlers — so that 'knights of industry' originally named men distinguished in fraud — citing the Abbé Galiani's claim, 'formally' written in 1750, that no fortune could grow without other fortunes being diminished? Quote the passage that gives the INTUS-STRUO derivation and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus argues against the doctrine that industrial progress flows from scientific theory by stacking three biographical proofs in succession — that 'the barber Arkwright' invented the spinning machine 'with no scientific instruction' and judged 'only by groping, by trying, by experimenting'; that Newcomen and Cawley, two of the first to draw useful effects from the steam engine, were respectively a hardware dealer and a blacksmith; and that Watt himself, when in 1764 the University of Glasgow charged him with repairing a Newcomen machine, did so 'simply in the capacity of a craftsman,' having studied only until sixteen at a Scottish 'Grammar school'? Quote the relevant passages and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does the author illustrate his warning that 'overly adventurous speculators' do more harm to industry than mere dissipators by recounting that 'a great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes' — adding that this lord ardently wished to excite his peasants by example, and declared 'that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it' — at which the chemist replied 'God forbid that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses'? Quote the anecdote and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus contrasts French luxury manufactures with English production for the masses by claiming that 'a London brewer, M. Perkins, alone sells for nearly as much in beer as the three hundred and seventy-six butchers of Paris annually buy in meat at the markets of Sceaux and Poissy' — pairing this with the figures that the Paris butchers buy 45 million worth of meat while Perkins sells 40 million worth of beer, and that England, 'with a population that is not half that of France,' annually fabricates twice as many woolens, three times more cottons, and six times more iron or cast iron? Quote the passage that gives the Perkins / 376-butchers / Sceaux-and-Poissy comparison, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the verse work in the corpus whose opening describes a Roman emperor encamped on the bank of the Danube against a hostile alliance specifically named as 'the Hunnish people and Hrethgoths,' 'Franks and Hugas,' and which then narrates the same emperor's pre-battle dream of a heavenly herald revealing a gem-adorned cross inscribed with letters of light bearing the message 'By this sign, conquer.' Quote the named-tribes line and the cross-inscription verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the verse work in the corpus in which a queen, after starving a reluctant Hebrew informant for seven nights in a dry well, is led to a hill where the man digs 'twenty feet deep' and unearths 'three crosses together' from a dark cavern; the queen then has all three set up in the city until a dead youth is brought on a bier at the ninth hour and revives only when the third cross is raised over his body. Quote the depth-and-three-crosses passage and the corpse-revival passage verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the verse work in the corpus whose closing passage laments an old man 'tossed by waves of care' who once 'in the mead-hall received treasures, appled gold' and rode where 'the horse measured the mile-paths before him, proudly prancing, adorned with wires,' and which then transitions into an eschatological fire-judgment that sorts every soul into exactly three ranks within the flame — the righteous 'uppermost in that fire,' the sinful tormented 'in the middle, covered with soot,' and the accursed cast 'in the fire's depths' down into hell's abyss. Quote the 'appled gold' mead-hall line and the three-tier fire-judgment passage verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does a writer answer the censor's panic over revolutionary atrocity by listing — without naming the country — the spectacle of a 'gentle people' so debased that it presses more eagerly to executions than to actors, carries about torn limbs of its fellow citizens still dripping and steaming amid songs of jubilation, and whose children spin bloody heads instead of tops, and then insist that these horrors are not the fruits of freedom of thought but the consequences of previous long intellectual slavery? Quote the passage with the bloody-heads-as-tops image and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does an author satirize princely truth-by-fiat by recommending that rulers imitate the early Christian emperors who altered doctrine with each new reign — and propose that, since the people have grown so accustomed to the prince's having willed that 'one equals three' that they now believe they discovered it themselves, the prince should now command, 'just once,' that one equals one — not because the opposite contradicts itself, but because he wills it? Quote the passage and name the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does a writer urge a thought-experiment in which one clothes a man of straw in royal attire, places the scepter in his stuffed hand, and seats him on the throne — asking whether the approaching subject's back would be any less supple, his demeanor any less respectful, his words any less timid — to prove that the reverence shown to a prince is owed to the office, not the person? Quote the passage and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that gives a month-by-month embryology with this exact sequence of transformations: in the first month a clot of blood the color of a crushed piece of spleen; in the third month the spleen-like mass becomes a single red egg of blood; in the fourth month the egg becomes a Śivaliṅga, hollow in the middle, inhabited by Oṃkāra and the subtle form; in the fifth month the Śivaliṅga becomes an illusory form; in the sixth month the illusory form becomes fire; in the seventh month the fire becomes 'like an ivory doll'; in the eighth month breath emerges from Oṃkāra together with flesh, bone, nails, and hair; and at ten months the yoga is complete and the child emerges from the womb. Quote the embryological sequence verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that teaches a five-syllable mantra called the Pañcakāṇḍa in which each syllable is assigned to one of the five gross elements with this exact mapping: in the syllable jaḥ, earth is to be known; in the syllable taḥ, the waters are established; in the syllable kiḥ is the great fire; in the syllable uṃ one should place the wind; and joined with the syllable phaṭ for the ether — recited for the destruction of great sins, granting the world of Śiva to the wise reciter. Quote the two ślokas that give this element-to-syllable mapping verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that closes with a purification-and-exorcism rite addressed to a son of Śiva who is depicted in a magical drawing with a discus in his left hand and a mace in his right, with these exact ritual materials: ivory bamboo (ampel gading) bearing the rajah of the deity, a white-and-yellow ajuman set, a sucī offering with a white crested chicken as its main dish, water held in a copper vessel (sanku), sudhāmala flowers with pras and santun offerings, a monetary offering of eleven hundred, and samsam of katima leaves; and that lists the rite's protections as effective against an epidemic, epilepsy, spirit affliction, and destruction caused by animals. Quote the deity's invocation that hails him as 'Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī, who makes opposing diseases faint, who gives life to the three worlds,' and identify the work and chapter.

Find the ghazal in the corpus whose speaker, complaining of a sorrow that needs a wholly new cure, dismisses the Son of Mary as insufficient ('Let someone be the Son of Mary, great and true, what can one do? / For this my sorrow, find a cure that's new, what can one do?') and, later in the same poem, asks pointedly what the guide Khizr ever accomplished for Alexander, concluding that there is therefore no one left to follow. Quote both couplets verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Locate the ghazal in the corpus that opens with the metaphysical paradox 'When nothing was, then God would be; if nothing were, God still would be,' immediately follows it with the speaker's complaint that his own being has 'drowned' him ('had I not been, what would there be?'), and contains a surreal couplet imagining the speaker's severed head laid 'gently, grieving' on his own knee. Quote the opening paradox and the severed-head couplet verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the ghazal in the corpus where the speaker dismisses 'the fabled throne of Solomon' as 'but a piece of play' and the Savior's miracle as 'a word I brush away,' and which contains a separate couplet declaring that his Faith holds him back while Heresy would have its way, with 'the Kaaba at my back, the Church is on display.' Quote both couplets verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the patriotic anthem in the corpus, structured as four refrain-stanzas each ending with a line declaring 'this is my homeland,' that names a single country as the place where Chishti first delivered the call of Truth, where Nanak sang of Oneness, where the Tatars made a homeland, where Hijaz's sons came from the desert, where the wisest Greeks were left spellbound, and where Prophet Noah's ark first saw the light. Quote the opening quatrain and the closing quatrain verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Locate the riverside meditation in the corpus in which the speaker stands on the bank of a river at evening, sees in the distance 'the minarets of the Chughtai king's last sleep,' calls that palace 'a book from a lost history,' watches a swift boat with a boatman fighting cresting waves disappear from view, and ends by likening the human life to that boat — vanishing from sight but never perishing. Quote the Chughtai-king couplet and the closing couplet, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the dream-narrative poem in the corpus, voiced by a grieving mother, in which she sees a procession of boys 'in robes of emerald green' each carrying a burning lamp, recognises her own dead son walking at the back of the line with no lamp lit, reproaches him for abandoning her, and is told in reply that her own tears have extinguished the lamp. Quote the closing two-line answer of the child verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Somewhere in the corpus, the stars complain to the moon that they have grown weary of shining, and the moon answers that to pause on the cosmic path is a flaw because 'hidden in Stillness is death's own law.' Identify the work and chapter, and quote the moon's reply about motion and stillness verbatim.

Find the elegy in the corpus that mourns a Mediterranean island as the 'grave of Hejazi civilization,' compares the speaker's lament to the nightingale of Shiraz weeping for Baghdad and to Ibn Badrun's anguish over Granada, and ends with the speaker promising to carry the island's sorrow 'as a gift to Hindustan.' Quote the closing couplet and identify the chapter.

Where in the corpus does a Muslim poet praise a Hindu sannyasi by fusing the Islamic shahada with a pearl-in-river image — declaring that 'In the river of "La" lies hidden the pearl of "illa-Allah"' — and pairing it with the figure of 'the Abraham of Love' shattering 'the idol of the Self'? Quote both couplets and name the work and chapter.

Identify the poem in the corpus that frames the human creature as a riddle 'hidden from his own gaze' and contrasts him with self-content rivers, winds, stars and a setting sun that 'drinks the cup of twilight,' closing on the lament that nobody shares the bitterness of human existence. Quote the closing couplet and give the chapter.

Somewhere in the corpus a flower bud is described as drinking morning light from 'the Sun's own goblet' and tearing its breast open before the sun — the speaker then prays to be 'wrapped within' the sun's beams 'like a bud.' Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.

Find the ghazal in the corpus where a wave and a pearl debate the source of nobility — the wave saying its honor is in journeying, the pearl insisting honor lies in staying enclosed — and the same poem later claims that if you pricked a rose's vein with a needle, 'a drop of human blood' would fall. Quote both couplets and identify the chapter.

Locate the poem in the corpus that addresses a solitary heart by telling it the night sky is its companion, that 'These lovely pearls, so bright they are, / Are but your tears, become a star,' and that Nature itself is the heart's kindred spirit. Quote the tear-into-star couplet and identify the work and chapter.

Find the opening ghazal in the corpus that catalogues divine self-manifestation across opposites — 'Your height in the heavens, Your lowliness in the earth's deep core, / Your current in the ocean, Your stillness on the waiting shore' — and then claims that what is awake in the conscious soul sleeps 'in the tree, the flower, the beast, the stone, and in the distant star.' Quote both couplets and identify the chapter.

Find the verse passage that names a former 'abode of Caesar' as an 'eternal symbol of the Ummah's power and grandeur', and that hears 'a voice arises' from the tomb of Ayyub Ansari proclaiming the city the very heart of the Islamic nation. Identify the work and chapter and quote the relevant lines verbatim.

Locate the long verse complaint addressed to God that, in a single rhetorical stretch, asks who tore out 'Khyber's mighty door', who 'laid Caesar's city flat upon the floor', who smashed the man-made idols, and who 'extinguished Zoroaster's sacred fire'. Identify the work and chapter and quote the stanza verbatim.

Find the short anthem-like verse that opens 'From Chin to Arab lands, our story flows' and proceeds to apostrophise both 'O Garden of Andalusia' (asking whether it remembers when 'on your branches high our nest in safety lay') and 'O wave of Tigris' (saying the river itself tells the speaker's story). Identify the work and chapter and quote those two apostrophes verbatim.

Where in the corpus does a bound demon, forced to confess his career of corruption, enumerate in a single speech that he whispered to Herod's heart to behead John for rebuking an unlawful marriage, taught Simon to insult Christ's chosen thanes as sorcerers, drove Nero with snares to kill Peter and Paul, was the teacher behind Pilate's crucifixion of the Ruler of skies, and likewise taught Aegeas to hang the holy Andrew on a high beam? Quote the catalog verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus describes a persecutor ordering an earthen clay vessel filled with lead to be set atop the greatest of pyres, a 'bath' boiled hot, into which the saint is shoved guiltless — only for the lead to spring wide and burn precisely seventy-five of the heathen host, while the holy one stands unharmed in the flame, her veil, robe, hair, skin and limbs untouched by fire? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus does a defeated heathen lord, after the saint's beheading, flee by ship onto 'the swan's road' only to drown with his retinue mid-voyage — and the poet pointedly numbers thirty-four of his warrior-kin lost to the wave's surge, lamenting that those thanes in the deep abyss could no longer expect their treasure-giver to grant them rings in the wine-hall or 'apple-gold from the beer-bench'? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus assigns each of the five Tathāgatas a specific internal organ as his bodily abode — placing Vairocana inside the heart, Akṣobhya inside the lungs, Ratnasambhava inside the spleen, Amitābha inside the liver, and Amoghasiddhi inside the gall bladder — with each chapter ending on the formula 'as for his residence in the body, the inside of the [organ] is his abode'? Quote the residence-formulas for at least three of the five Tathāgatas verbatim and identify the work and chapters.

Which work in the corpus, after returning the five Tathāgatas to their lokadhātus and reinstalling them inside the practitioner's body as the five wisdoms (pañcajñāna) and the five senses (pañcendriya), assigns Akṣobhya to the eyes, Ratnasambhava to the ears, Amitābha to the nose, Amoghasiddhi to the mouth, and concludes that 'he who is the sense of taste is Lord Vairocana, residing in the heart, he is the mind' — calling the resulting fivefold inner constellation the 'Divine Five-Souls' (Pañcātma) which form the self? Quote the sense-to-Tathāgata mapping and the closing Pañcātma identification verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus opens by declaring that the divine Buddha was in the great dissolution (mahāpralaya), that 'his body came into being, he then became the Lord Pañcatathāgata' — and then describes the central Tathāgata of this fivefold body as wielding the cakra as his implement, riding a lion as his mount, ruling Sahāvatī as the name of his dominion, and accompanied by the three goddesses Sattvavajrī, Dharmavajrī, and Karmavajrī, with the bodhyagrī mudrā and the wisdom called śāśvata? Quote the cosmogonic opening (Buddha-in-mahāpralaya becoming the Pañcatathāgata) and the central Tathāgata's lion-mount / cakra-implement / Sahāvatī-dominion attributes verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

In the corpus, which text enumerates a woman's separate property as six-fold and, within that scheme, names a category called pādavandanika — the gift made for bowing at the feet of one's mother-in-law or father-in-law — and groups it together with what is given out of affection (prītidatta)? Quote the verse that introduces the six-fold list and the verse that defines pādavandanika, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus defines bride-price (śulka) as 'whatever value is received for household utensils, vehicles, milch animals, ornaments, and servants,' and elsewhere caps the gift of woman's separate property by relatives at two thousand while explicitly forbidding immovable property in such gifts? Quote both verses verbatim and give the work and the chapter for each.

In the corpus, which work declares a category of impartible wealth called dhvajāhṛta — literally 'what is won under the banner' — and defines it as what is brought from battle after routing the enemy's army, having risked one's life for one's master? Quote the verse that introduces and glosses dhvajāhṛta and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus in which an ascetic on a hell-tour notices a copper cauldron being scrubbed clean in the courtyard of the Lord of Death and asks why an empty vessel is being washed. The Lord of Death answers that a great sinner is expected within seven nights, that his term in that cauldron will run for a hundred thousand years, and that afterwards he will be carried by weapon-birds to be impaled on an iron tree and then hung head-down from a bamboo pole over a fire on the iron plain. Quote the lines in which the visitor asks about the empty cauldron, and the lines in which the Lord of Death gives the seven-nights-and-hundred-thousand-years answer. Identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that describes a hell-torment reversed by meditation: on the tenth night a sinner holds a pure mind in the cauldron, the cauldron shatters and its fire dies, becoming a jeweled lotus pond, while the nearby sword-tree is transmuted into a Pārijāta whose fruits are flawless Kaustubha jewels and whose flowers are golden necklaces, its blood-sap turned exceedingly sweet-smelling. Quote the verses in which the cauldron becomes the jeweled lotus pond and in which the sword-tree becomes the Pārijāta with Kaustubha-jewel fruits. Identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus in which a Buddha-figure, instructing a pupil on the origin of the body, says that the seed emerged from the father's liṅga 'like molten tin, poured out,' that Kāma is its name at that moment while Ratih is the name when it clung to the mother, and that the embryo then took form in 'the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb' — paired with a five-souls scheme (ātmā, parātmā, antarātmā, nirātmā, cetanātmā) joined to the five great elements. Quote the verse with the molten-tin / Kāma-and-Ratih image, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus opens with a cosmogony in which a divine being splits a golden egg in two to form heaven and earth, then produces the four social orders from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet? Quote the relevant verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus are eight named forms of marriage enumerated together — including a 'voluntary union of a maiden and her lover' springing from desire, a forcible abduction after kinsmen are slain, and a clandestine approach to a sleeping or intoxicated girl as the most wicked? Quote the verses naming the eight forms and identify the work and chapter.

Which work personifies the rod of punishment as the Lord's own son, created at the beginning as Dharma itself, and warns that without it 'the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit'? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work prescribes a graduated penalty for theft scaled by social order — eightfold guilt for the lowest order, sixteenfold for the next, thirty-twofold for the warrior, and sixty-fourfold (or up to twice that) for the priestly class — explicitly because the priest 'is a knower of the good and bad qualities of that act'? Quote the verses and identify the work and chapter.

Where in the corpus are specific bodily disfigurements catalogued as the karmic signatures of past sins — a gold thief getting diseased nails, a liquor-drinker black teeth, a slanderer a stinking nose, a clothes-thief vitiligo, a horse-thief lameness? Quote the verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work supplies a long list of stolen items paired with the specific animal birth they cause — stealing grain produces a rat, bronze a swan, milk a crow, ghee a mongoose, meat a vulture, salt a cricket, silk a partridge, a cow an iguana? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work pairs the doctrine that Dharma loses one of its four feet in each successive cosmic age with an age-specific 'highest dharma' — austerity in the first age, knowledge in the second, sacrifice in the third, and giving alone in the last? Quote the verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work argues that animals slain at a sacrifice attain higher rebirths and that 'in a sacrifice, killing is not killing,' while in the same chapter declaring that one who kills an animal needlessly will himself suffer a violent death in as many future births as the slain animal had hairs? Quote both verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus, when describing how a judge should track the truth from circumstantial clues, compares the procedure to a hunter who follows a wounded deer by the trail of blood it leaves in the thicket? Quote the line verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

In the corpus, which text argues that even a matter seen with one's own eyes still requires careful examination — illustrating the point with the paired claim that 'the sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire,' yet there is in fact no surface in the sky and no fire in a firefly? Quote the verse and give the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus declares that witnesses are of exactly eleven kinds — five appointed (a written witness, a reminded witness, an accidental witness, a secret witness, and a subsequent witness) and six unappointed? Quote the verse that gives the eleven/five/six count and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus contains a stanzaic catalogue of misfortunes — Weland bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters, Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy, Mæðhild and Geat, Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold, the wolf-minded Eormanric ruling the Goths — each stanza closing with the same one-line refrain of consolation, before the speaker finally names himself as a scop displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda? Quote at least three occurrences of the refrain together with the speaker's self-naming, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus contemplates a shattered city as the 'work of giants', describing roofs toppled and a barred gate plundered, and then turns specifically to the city's many bath-houses — stone courts where 'a stream threw up heat in a wide surge', a wall enclosing all 'within its bright embrace' where the baths were 'hot at their heart', with hot streams poured 'over the grey stone' until 'the round pool grew hot'? Quote the bath passage and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus contains a brief, riddle-like first-person lament in which the speaker says her people 'will devour him, should he dare to come in a raiding troop', insists 'our fates are not the same', describes her beloved as on one island while she is on another 'fast' island 'by fen surrounded', then suddenly turns to address a second man by name, telling him that 'our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods'? Quote the address to the named second man and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus distinguishes two specific kinds of sons born of another man's wife — naming the one born while the husband is still alive a 'Kuṇḍa' and the one born after the husband's death a 'Golaka'? Quote the verse that defines this pair and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus warns that the householder from whose house an *atithi* (guest) turns away with shattered hopes will see his ancestors refuse to eat his offerings for fifteen years, and that his fire-offering becomes as useless as 'a thousand loads of wood and a hundred pots of clarified butter'? Quote the verses verbatim and give the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus assigns the names 'Gaurī', 'Rohiṇī', and 'Kanyā' to a girl in her eighth, ninth, and tenth years respectively (after which she is called *rajasvalā*), and then declares that if a father fails to give her in marriage by her twelfth year, his own ancestors drink her menstrual blood month after month? Quote the verses that give the age-by-age naming and the twelfth-year sanction, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus identifies each of the five Tathāgatas with a specific state of the practitioner's breath — naming the breath in the right nostril as Amitābha, the breath in the left as Amoghasiddhi, the breath exiting both at once as Ratnasambhava, the breath not exiting from either side as Akṣobhya, and the culmination of the breath as Vairocana — and calls these five 'the fivefold culmination of stainless purity' or 'the Five Sacred Tastes,' locating their abodes at the tip of the nose, the forehead, and the crown of the head? Quote the five-line breath-to-Tathāgata mapping verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus teaches a yogic ritual catalogue in which the breath in the right nostril becomes a triangular fire-maṇḍala, blazing red, with a trident at its center (a means for all actions); or, alternatively, a square Mahendra-maṇḍala blazing gold-yellow with an open five-pronged vajra at its center (for increase of life and wealth); while the breath in the left becomes a formless wind-maṇḍala, black, green, or yellow, blazing at both ends, with a banner-symbol and a hook and ring in the left hand for attraction, immobilization, and expulsion; or, alternatively, a circular white water-maṇḍala (bāruṇamaṇḍala) with a pure-crystal subtle maṇḍala at its center for pacification (śāntika)? Quote the descriptions of the fire-maṇḍala and the water-maṇḍala verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus, in defining the supreme grade of giving (above ordinary giving and above the giving of one's wife and child), tells of the Mahāsattva who gave his flesh and blood as a gift to demons, tigers, and garuḍas; gave his eyes as a gift to an old, blind brahmin who came to test his heroic generosity; and gave his heart as a gift to a suffering, sorrowful creature — illustrating a verse that says one who has given one's own flesh and blood and conquered the lord of demons, the heart, and the body, and through the power of giving experiences no pain, is what the sages proclaim 'mahātidāna'? Quote the verse and the three specific recipients (demons-tigers-garuḍas / old blind brahmin / suffering creature) verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus, structured as a guru-to-disciple instruction in which a 'revered son' is the questioner, that maps the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, the fourth) onto the four great Vedic śrauta sacrifices in this exact order: jāgrat = aśvamedha, svapna = vājapeya, suṣupti = puṇḍarīka, tūrya = rājasūya. Quote the verse that makes this four-to-four mapping verbatim, identify the work and chapter, and quote the prose gloss that follows.

Find the work in the corpus that contains a brief debate about the size of the gall bladder in which a 'great fool' is paraphrased as arguing that, although it is said to be a thumb's breadth in size, the gall bladder is in fact 'as large as the seven islands' — a statement the text frames as 'the argument of a pandit.' Quote the verse and the prose gloss verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that compares the Self trapped in worldly suffering to a serpent submerged in terrifying waters, and identifies the sacred syllable oṃ as the Garuḍa that swoops down to lift the serpent-Self up to the abode of Shiva. Quote the verse that names the puruṣa as nāga and oṃkāra as Garuḍa, and the prose line that says oṃkāra brings the Self to śivapada. Identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus maps the human body onto the seven mountains, seven oceans, and seven continents with specific organ-by-organ identifications — equating the kidneys with Mount Mālyavān, the heart with Mount Mahāmeru, the liver with Mount Vindhya, the spleen with Mount Gandhamādana; and equating urine with the fresh-water ocean, blood with the ocean of sugarcane juice, sweat with the salt ocean, fat with the oil ocean, saliva with the honey ocean, marrow with the milk ocean, and the brain with the ocean of curds; while bone is Jambudvīpa, sinew is Kuśadvīpa, flesh is Śaṅkhadvīpa, skin is Śālmalidvīpa, and teeth are Krauñcadvīpa? Quote the equations of the seven oceans and seven continents verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus describes a graded fourteen-step cosmic devolution in which a being, 'lacking in yoga,' falls down a fixed ladder of births — Tripuruṣa becoming Pañcaṛṣi, Pañcaṛṣi becoming Saptaṛṣi, Saptaṛṣi becoming Devaṛṣi, then Devatā, Vidyādhara, Gandharva, Dānava, Daitya, Rākṣasa, Bhūta Yakṣa, Bhūta Dĕṅĕn, Bhūta Kāla, Bhūta Piśāca, finally human, and at last a being of the triyak realm — and immediately afterwards classifies the five kinds of triyak as pāśu (village domestic animal), mṛga (forest wild beast), pakṣī (bird), mīna (fish in water), and pipīlikā (the belly-mover whose 'other names are not known')? Quote the descent ladder verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus assigns three classes of named blades to three patron divinities by social function — declaring that the Rākṣasa is the patron divinity of the king's life-taking weapons such as the curik, malalya, and śaṅkuh; that the Daitya is the patron divinity of the farmer's livelihood-producing implements such as the luke and pṛkul (or prĕkul); and that the Dānava is the patron divinity of the priest's ritual knives such as the pamiso (or pakisi), kartrī, and karayu — and immediately frames this within a blacksmith simile in which raw iron, tongs, hammer, anvil, and file all answer to the smith's will, the blade alone striking good and evil while the tools do not? Quote the three blade-divinity assignments verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus illustrates the doctrine that virtues and vices arise from association by telling of two parrots named Gāvākṣa and Girikā — one raised by a hunter and the other by a scholar — where a king who has lost his retinue is first brought to the hunter's house and the parrot Girikā cries out 'Come on, come on! Smash his head!' while the scholar's parrot Gāvākṣa instead welcomes the king with words of greeting, offers him a new mat to sit on, betel nut and fresh betel leaf with uncooked lime to chew, and invites him to bathe in the spring? Quote the parrots' two contrasting speeches verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus argues that nothing is without a flaw by listing a catalogue that includes the lotus being full of thorns, the snowy mountain having its cold, the sandalwood tree being inhabited by serpents in its hollows, the moon's body stained by a hare, the ocean's salty water — and then turns to the gods themselves: that Viṣṇu was a cowherd, that Indra is exceedingly fickle, and that Śaṅkara has a blue throat from the kālakūṭa poison — concluding that if even the gods carry such defects, how much more so will people on earth? Quote the verse listing these specific flaws verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus illustrates the maxim that even a stranger can be a true kinsman and a kinsman can be an enemy — pairing the abstract claim that a disease born of the body is harmful while a forest herb that helps is a friend with a specifically named exemplum: that Sugrīva was pained by his own kinsman Bāli, yet sought out Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa, though strangers, in the middle of the forest, to serve as the medicine for Sugrīva's pain? Quote the verse and the named exemplum verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which dharmaśāstra digest opens by tracing its patron's dynasty back to a wrestler-king named Medinīmalla of the Sīkāśīrāja lineage—'a wrestler upon the earth with the weight of his mighty forces' whose 'fame [was] as white as the brilliance of the serpent king Ādiśeṣa'—and then continues the genealogy through Arjuna, Malakhāna, and Pratāparudra? Quote the verse that introduces Medinīmalla and name the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit work names its own author's Brahmin family as the 'Dūravāra lineage, a crest-jewel of the glorious Gopācala,' tracing the family from a learned ancestor called Haṃsa, then to a son who 'went in ancient times to the city of the enemy of the cities (Vārāṇasī)' and obtained 'the glorious Caṇḍīśvara, a jewel among Agnihotrins, as his weighty preceptor'? Quote the two verses that name the Dūravāra lineage of Gopācala and the journey to Vārāṇasī under Caṇḍīśvara, and identify the work and chapter.

Which dharma text repeatedly closes its sections with a colophon naming its compiler as the son of Paraśurāma Miśra and grandson of Haṃsa Paṇḍita, prompted by a king described as 'the sun causing the lotus of the earth encircled by the four oceans to bloom'—the son of Mahārāja Madhukarasāha and grandson of Pratāparudra—and praising the compiler as 'a lion to the great elephant of the world's poverty, the very life of the learned'? Quote one full instance of this colophon and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus opens with a deity who, after rescuing the Earth from the cosmic waters in the form of a Boar whose tusks are the sacrificial post and tongue is the sacrificial fire, vanishes — prompting the Earth-goddess to take the form of a beautiful woman with lips red as the Bandhujīva flower, seek out the sage Kashyapa, and at his direction travel to the Ocean of Milk to ask the reclining god 'what will be my support?' before being seated on a golden throne to receive the law? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.
