Which work in the corpus enumerates a sequence of named hells — beginning with Tāmisra (Darkness) and Andhatāmisra (Blinding Darkness), passing through Raurava (Howling) and Mahāraurava (Great Howling), Kālasūtra (the Black Thread), Saṃjīvana (Reviving), Avīci (Waveless), Kuḍmala (Bud), Pūtimṛttika (Putrid Clay), Lohaśaṅku (Iron Spike), Kaṇṭakaśālmali (Thorny Silk-Cotton Tree), Asipatravana (Forest of Sword-leaves), and ending at Lohacāraka (Iron Cage) — and assigns durations of a Kalpa for heinous sinners and a Manvantara for great sinners? Quote the named-hell list and the duration verses and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus closes by having the deity address the Earth-goddess by name — 'O Dhara' — and praise the law-treatise just delivered as 'spoken by the god himself,' calling it a 'destroyer of bad dreams,' an 'abode of auspiciousness,' to be recited at śrāddhas by men who desire prosperity? Quote the closing verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work specifies an exact traveler's allowance for an ascetic short of provisions on a journey, permitting him to take 20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, one handful of rice, 1 jackfruit, 5 kalvi, and likewise sentul fruit from an abandoned garden — without being called a thief? Quote the passage verbatim and name the work and chapter.

Which work names five 'primary' outcastes — the liquor-seller, the trapper (or insect-burner), the butcher (life-taker), the potter, and the goldsmith (metal-burner) — and declares that their stalls and houses must be avoided by an ascetic because they are 'worse than the eighteen caṇḍālas'? Quote the verse and the commentary's enumeration verbatim, and name the work and chapter.

Which work warns an ascetic that, if he fails to protect his moral conduct, his own sacred thread will become a club to beat him in Yama's world, his sacred ash will become a mountain of fire burning his body, and his rosary will become an instrument of punishment with 'a hundred years of punishment for each bead'? Quote the passage verbatim and name the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus contains a passage in which the divine instructor — speaking in the first person to his pupil — confesses his own back-story: he names himself the seventh of an eight-fold succession of teaching deities (Ananta, Sūkṣma, Śivatama, Ekarudra, Ekanetra, Trimūrti, Śrīkaṇṭha, Śikhaṇḍī) in which each successor replaces the previous, identifies himself as 'that Śrīkaṇṭha,' and admits that having been struck by passion he gazed upon the god of love so that the body of Kāmadeva 'was shattered, incinerated, and turned to ash' — yet 'the passion remained in me,' which is why he took Lady Umā as his wife and fathered the holy Sanatkumāra? Quote the succession-list and the Kāmadeva-and-Umā confession verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus likens the cosmic veil of illusion to a honeycomb crowded with souls — declaring that 'māyā is like the honeycomb, and the ātmā is like the young bees,' that they are 'face-down, which means they are bent over,' and that each soul 'looks only downward, unaware of the tattva above it'? The same passage explains that this honey-comb soul-state arises because Śiva-tattva, originally crystal-clear, becomes stained by mala (impurity) and so 'ceases to be all-knowing and all-doing,' at which point it is renamed ātmā, meaning 'dazed consciousness.' Quote the honeycomb-and-young-bees image verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which work in the corpus stages an elephant-and-blind-men parable in a teacher-pupil dialogue, in which the questioner asks why his teacher has bestowed so many different scriptures (naming the three paths Śaiva, Pāśupata, and Alepaka), and is answered with a passage that compares confused seekers to a group of blind men feeling different parts of an elephant — one calling it a water jar, one a winnowing fan, one a snake, one a granary, one an eel, one a pillar — and then coins the technical term andhatattva ('the principle of the blind men') to name the resulting state of bewilderment? Quote the six blind-men-comparisons verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the legal-procedure chapter in the corpus that names exactly five ordeals — the balance, fire, water, poison, and the sacred libation — and then prescribes their procedural details with these specific numbers: an iron ball of fifty palas placed in the accused's hands, who must walk through seven circles each of sixteen finger-breadths, with seven Aśvattha leaves bound on his hands; for the poison ordeal the accused must eat 'the Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains'; and for the sacred-libation ordeal the accused is pure if no calamity befalls him within fourteen days. Quote the verse that addresses the Balance as 'O Mother' (asking to be carried up if pure and taken down if a sinner), and identify the work and chapter.

Find the legal text in the corpus that lays down a graduated monthly interest schedule with this exact dual structure: when there is a pledge, the lawful interest is one-eightieth of the principal per month; otherwise, it is two, three, four, or five percent according to the order of the four varṇas; and that further allows ten percent for those who travel through forests and twenty percent for those who travel by sea. The same passage also caps the maximum recoverable interest at eight times the principal for liquids, four times for cloth, three times for grain, and twice the principal for gold. Quote the verses fixing both the per-varṇa monthly rate and the forest/sea traveller rate, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the work in the corpus that, inside a chapter on expiation, gives a precise anatomical inventory of the human body with these specific numbers: a man's body has three hundred and sixty bones, sixty-four teeth with their sockets, twenty nails, seventy-two ribs (with sockets and nodules), forty-five bones in the back, fifteen in the neck, and seventeen in the chest; the body contains seven hundred veins, nine hundred sinews, two hundred arteries, and five hundred muscles; it has one hundred and seven vital spots and two hundred joints; and the body hairs together with the sweat ducts number 'fifty crores and four crores… and sixty-seven and a half lakhs.' Quote the verse declaring 'three hundred and sixty bones' and the verse giving 'seven hundred veins, nine hundred sinews, two hundred arteries, and five hundred muscles,' and identify the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes a fourfold-value fine for a very specific list of grocery adulterations — pepper mixed with stone fragments, salt mixed with earth, and asafoetida mixed with tree resin — placed alongside the related rule that ghee sold under the name of ghee but adulterated with sesame oil (or oil with ghee) likewise incurs a fourfold penalty on the merchant? Quote the two verses listing these adulterations verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus declares that for the convergence of all sins together no ordinary penance is known, and that the prescribed remedy in such a case is to take up the skull-bowl and observe the Kāpālika vow — explicitly named by the wise as the ultimate penance — even though for each single sin committed alone the wise know a separate penance? Quote the paired verses on the convergence of sins and the skull-bowl vow verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes that the king should banish from the kingdom a Brahmin who has abandoned his sacred fire by first smearing the man's body with ashes and fumigating him with the hair and bones of a corpse — and, in an immediately adjacent verse, makes 'the man who violates convention' go naked after being fanned with the hair of a corpse and his head adorned with a garland of corpse-hair? Quote both verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.

Find the ghazal in the corpus whose closing signature couplet is a poet's modest concession that he never inherited the elder Delhi master Mir's particular style ('I never attained Mir's manner / mode'), spoken in self-deprecation against fellow-poets who 'struck hard' at the same ghazal. The same poem contains a couplet boasting that the speaker's sigh-arrow struck Saturn's eye in the heavens, and a couplet declaring that from Love's hand neither Qais nor Farhad escaped — 'one struck down in the desert, the other on the mountain.' Quote the Mir-naming maqta and the Saturn couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Locate the ghazal in the corpus whose opening couplet (matla) treats a small, ordinary domestic gift — two almonds someone has sent tucked into a pouch / purse — as an offering for which the speaker pledges to give his eyes in return as religious duty. The same ghazal contains a later couplet declaring that no perfect vessel can ever come off the potter's wheel, so one should not waste this heart-dust on the potter's hand. Quote the almonds-in-a-pouch matla and the potter's-wheel couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.

Find the ghazal in the corpus whose every couplet ends on the same short refrain 'is love' (radif: '-aar ka' in the original), in which one couplet declares that even the heart's fire will not be extinguished beneath the dust of the grave — and that therefore a tall chinar tree will grow upon the speaker's tomb. The same ghazal contains a couplet calling the bittersweet juice of teardrops 'the sherbet of the pomegranate of the highest paradise (khuld-e-bareen),' and closes with the poet's signature warning that this tavern has no place for the wise, so one should flee the world. Quote the chinar-on-the-grave couplet and the pomegranate-of-paradise couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
